description: A DID listed in `audience[]` of a `private` context CAN retrieve the context by known ctx_id (HTTP 200), per RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.5 and RFC-ACDP-0002 §7. This is the retrieval half of the private/audience asymmetry; vis-005 covers the search half. The asymmetry is intentional: `audience` on a private context expresses 'you may read this if I send you the link', not 'you may discover this'.

scenarios.name: Audience member (in audience[]) retrieves private context by known ctx_id
scenarios.notes: `audience` grants retrieval access on private contexts — the audience member knows the ctx_id (out-of-band) and may fetch the body. This is the explicit RFC-ACDP-0002 §7 / RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.5 behavior. Contrast with vis-005, which forbids search visibility for the same requester.

scenarios.name: Outsider (NOT producer, NOT in audience) retrieves private context
scenarios.notes: Existence-leak prevention (RFC-ACDP-0008 §3.5): the response MUST be byte-indistinguishable from a request for a genuinely missing ctx_id. This scenario is included for completeness; vis-001 covers the existence-leak invariant in depth for restricted visibility, and the same invariant holds for private.

scenarios.name: Contributor (NOT in audience) retrieves private context
scenarios.notes: `contributors` is for attribution, not authorization (RFC-ACDP-0002 §7, RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.5). To grant a contributor read access on a private context, the producer MUST list the DID in `audience` explicitly — and even then, only retrieval is granted; search remains agent_id-only.
